questions.questionText: With reference to landholding systems in the Mauryan Empire as described in Kautilya’s Arthashastra, what was Sita land?

options.a: Land owned by village communities and free from state interference
options.b: Land granted to Brahmanas and religious institutions as tax-free endowments
options.c: Land reclaimed from forests and placed under direct state control
options.d: Land controlled by former tribal oligarchies that paid regular tribute to the state

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● In the **Arthashastra,** **Kautilya** employs the term ‘**Swayam’** to denote **proprietary rights** associated with the **sale and purchase of land.** During the Mauryan period, land was classified on the basis of ownership and was clearly demarcated and any attempt at encroachment was treated as a punishable offence. Kautilya discusses several categories of landholdings, among which two are particularly significant: **Rashtra lands and Sita lands.**
● **Rashtra lands** were territories that had earlier **belonged to tribal oligarchies** or republican polities
which were subdued prior to the Mauryan expansion. These regions were not under direct state control; their principal obligation to the Mauryan state was the payment of taxes or tribute.
● **Sita lands,** on the other hand, consisted of **areas reclaimed for cultivation by clearing forests,** often with the assistance of **forest-dwelling communities.** Such lands were placed under strict **state** **ownership and supervision**. They could neither be sold nor permanently transferred, although they might be leased to cultivators without conferring rights of alienation. **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.questionText:
Consider the following information:
Mahajanapada Capital Present Day Region
I. - Anga Champa Bhagalpur
II. - Kosala Vaishali Muzaffarpur
III. Avanti Pooncha Jammu
IV. Chedi Shuktimati Bundelkhand
In which of the above rows is the given information correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **sixth century BCE** is known as an era of **‘Second Urbanisation’** in the Indian Subcontinent. After the decline of the Harappan towns, and nearly a thousand years later, urban centres emerged again, but not in the Indus plain. The centre of economic and political activity shifted from the **North-West,** **Punjab, Haryana, and Western UP to Eastern UP and Bihar (Ganga Basin).**
● The use of better iron tools and weapons enabled some territorial states to become very large, and they came to be called **Mahajanapadas** (large territorial states) in North India (cities and states became visible in South India a few centuries later). Most of the Mahajanapadas were situated in the north of the Vindhyas, between Bihar in the east and the north-west frontier of the subcontinent.
**State** **Capital**
**Anga** (Modern districts of Monghyr and **Bhagalpur, Bihar**).
**Champa** (located at the confluence of the Ganga and Champa rivers). **So, row I is correctly matched.**
**Kosala (Eastern U.P.,** included **Ayodhya and the** **tribal republican territory of** **the Shakyas of Kapilavastu)**
Sarayu River divided the state into two parts: Northern part’s capital: **Shravasti** and Southern part’s capital: **Kushavati.** **So,** **row II is not correctly matched.**
Cheti or **Chedi (Eastern part of** **Bundelkhand in Central India)**
**Shuktimati** or Sotthivatinagara. **So, row IV is correctly** **matched.**
**Avanti (Central Malwa)**
Divided by the Vindhyas **North Avanti Ujjain** and South Avanti Mahishmati. **So, row III is not correctly matched.**
**Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to the Mughal Paintings:
I. - The paintings during Akbar’s reign were primarily focussed on popular art instead of court art.
II. 	 The paintings during Jahangir’s reign emphasised bringing naturalism to portrait painting.
III. 	 Aurangzeb encouraged the use of charcoal and pencil in drawing.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
The paintings made in the Mughal period had a distinctive style as they were drawings from **Persian** **antecedents.** There was a change in colour palette, themes and forms. The focus shifted from depicting the god to glorifying the ruler and showing his life.
● **Akbar** was responsible for the establishment of an entire department devoted to the paintings and scribing of his documents. He established a formal artistic studio called **Tasvir Khana,** where the artists were hired on a salary and they developed their own styles. During his period, the artists encouraged the **use of calligraphy in the paintings.** One of the distinguishing features of this period was the **transformation of popular art to court art**, i.e. the artist was more focused on **depicting the** **scenes of court life than the life of the masses.** Famous painters of this period include: Daswanth, Basawan and Kesu.  **So, statement I is not correct.**
● The Mughal paintings reached their zenith during the period of **Jahangir.** He was a naturalist by nature and preferred the paintings of flora and fauna, i.e. birds, animals, trees and flowers. **He shifted from** **illustrated manuscripts to albums and emphasised bringing naturalism to portrait** **(individual) painting.** One of the unique trends that developed in this period was of decorated margins around the paintings that were sometimes as elaborate as the paintings themselves. **So, statement II** **is correct.**
● The tenor of the Mughal paintings changed rapidly during the period of **Shah Jahan.** Shah Jahan liked **creating artificial elements** in the paintings. He eschewed the **use of charcoal** to draw and encouraged the artists to **draw and sketch using a pencil.** He also ordered an increase in the use of gold and silver in the paintings.  **So, statement III is not correct.**
● **Aurangzeb did not generally encourage painting** and as a result, a large number of Mughal court painters started migrating to the provincial courts in Rajasthan, etc. **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.questionText:
A whispering gallery is an architectural feature designed so that even the faintest sounds, like whispers, can travel clearly across its structure to distant points within the gallery.
Which of the following popular monuments have such structures?
I. - Gol Gumbaz
II. 	 Victoria Memorial
III. 	 Rani Ki Vav
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● A **whispering gallery** is an architectural feature designed so that even the faintest sounds, like whispers, can travel clearly across its structure to distant points within the gallery.  This acoustic phenomenon occurs due to the **curved or domed shape** of the interior, which allows sound waves to travel along the surface with minimal loss.
● In India, notable examples include the **Gol Gumbaz** in Bijapur, the **Victoria Memorial** in Kolkata, and the **Golghar Granary** in Bankipore.
○ **Gol Gumbaz** is the tomb of Mohammed Adil Shah (ruled 1627–1657) in **Vijayapura**. The attraction at the Gol Gumbaz is the **Whispering Gallery**, where even minute sounds can be heard clearly 37 metres away. **So point I is correct.** ○ **Victoria Memorial Hall** was **commissioned in 1906** by Lord Curzon, the then Viceroy of India, to commemorate **Queen Victoria** and was completed in **1921**. The circular gallery at the base of the inner dome, and the space between the two domes, form two very perfect whispering galleries. **So point II is correct.**
● While **Rani ki Vav** in Patan, Gujarat, is primarily celebrated as a UNESCO World Heritage **stepwell** known for its intricate sculptures and architectural precision, it is not designed to create a **whispering** **gallery-like effect**. **So, point III is not correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
I. - Malavikagnimitra, written by Kalidasa, depicts the love story of Agnimitra, the son of the founder of the Shunga dynasty, Pushyamitra Shunga.
II. 	 Swapnavasavadatta, written by Vishakhadutta, narrates the ascent of King Chandragupta Maurya to the power in Magadha.
III. 	 Meghadoota written by Sudraka, is a romantic comedy centered on the theme of loveintrigue of King Udayana of Kaushambi.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Kalidasa** is renowned for his three major plays, among which **Malavikagnimitra** portrays the love story of **Malavika,** a **maiden in the queen’s household** and Agnimitra, the **son of Pushyamitra** **Shunga.** **So, statement I is correct.**
● **Mudrarakshasa**, written by **Vishakhadatta**, is a political drama that narrates the rise of **Chandragupta Maurya to power in India,** while **Swapnavasavadatta**, composed by **Bhasa,** centres on the **story of Vasavadatta and the events revealed through a dream. So, statement** **II is not correct.**
● **Meghadoota**, composed by the celebrated Sanskrit poet and **dramatist Kalidasa** during the **Gupta** **period** (4th–6th century CE), is a lyrical poem that **narrates the poignant story of a Yaksha** who, while living in exile, implores a passing cloud to carry a message of love to his beloved wife from whom he is separated. **So, statement III is not correct.**
○ **Mricchakatika** (The Little Clay Cart), authored by Sudraka, is a celebrated Sanskrit play that centres on the love affair between the noble but impoverished Brahmin Charudatta and the wealthy courtesan Vasantasena. ○ **Ratnavali** (The Jewel Necklace) is a famous 7th-century Sanskrit drama  attributed to the Harshavardhana (Harsha). It is a romantic comedy centered on the theme of love-intrigue between King Udayana of Kaushambi and Princess Ratnavali of Simhala (Ceylon). **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.questionText: Which one of the following Mughal emperors conferred the title of ‘Raja’ upon Ram Mohan Roy?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Raja Ram Mohan Roy** was a leading social and religious reformer of nineteenth-century India and played a significant role in promoting modern education, rationalism and social reform.
● The **Mughal Emperor Akbar II** conferred upon him the **title ‘Raja’** and later sent him as an envoy to **England (1830)** to represent the Mughal court’s interests before the British government. This recognition reflected Roy’s stature as an intellectual intermediary between Indian society and the colonial state. His engagement with Western liberal ideas and Indian philosophical traditions enabled him to campaign effectively against practices such as Sati, child marriage and rigid caste distinctions. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box** **Raja Ram Mohan Roy (1772–1833):**
● He published the Persian journal **Mirat-ul-Akbar (1822)**, which focused on political, social and religious issues.
● He founded the Bengali newspaper **Sambad Kaumudi,** which became a powerful medium for social reform and public opinion.
● He established the **Brahmo Samaj (1828)** to promote monotheism, rational thinking and opposition to social evils such as caste discrimination and idol worship.
● He played a decisive role in the **abolition of Sati (1829)** by petitioning the British government and mobilizing public opinion after being personally affected by the practice.
● He founded the **Atmiya Sabha (1814),** an early reformist group that encouraged discussions on religious philosophy and ethical conduct.
● He was widely regarded as the ‘**Father of the Indian Renaissance’** for initiating modern reform movements in India.
● Freedom fighter Gopal Krishna Gokhale described him as the ‘Father of Modern India’.

questions.questionText: On which one of the following occasions did Mahatma Gandhi remark, “Gandhi may die, but Gandhism will remain forever” ?

options.a: During the launch of the Champaran Satyagraha, 1917
options.b: During the launch of the Dandi Salt March,
options.c: At Karachi Session of the Indian National Congress, 1931
options.d: At the Second Round Table Conference in London, 1931

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Mahatma Gandhi (1869–1948)** was the supreme leader of the Indian nationalist movement, guiding it for nearly three decades from **1917 to 1947.** He was not merely a political leader but also a profound thinker who questioned the dominant assumptions and doctrines of his time and offered meaningful alternatives. The term Gandhism signifies the **ideas, principles and practices associated with** **Gandhi.**
● The term “**Gandhism**” gained popularity after Gandhi’s address at the **Karachi Session of the Indian** **National Congress, 1931**, where he remarked, **“Gandhi may die, but Gandhism will live** **forever.”** It is believed that he coined the term on this occasion to express the philosophy underlying his lifelong commitment to truth and non-violence.
● **Gandhism** is a way of life. It represents a **moral attitude towards personal and social problems** and offers ancient wisdom as solutions to modern challenges. For Gandhi, society was his temple, service was his form of worship, and humanity was his supreme concern. Truth was his God and non-violence was the means to realize it. **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Work Author/ Editor
I. - Bharat Shramajeevi Dadabhai Naoroji
II. - Durgeshnandini Bankim Chandra Chatterjee
III. - Mahratta Vishnu Sakharam Khandekar
IV. - Nibandhamala Ramchandra Shukla
How many of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Bharat Shramajeevi was the pioneering journal specifically dedicated to the working class**. It played a pioneering role in the early Indian labour movement during the late 19th century. It was established and edited by **Sasipada Banerji**, a prominent Brahmo Samaj social reformer from Bengal. Its primary goal was to **highlight the poor working conditions of industrial laborers**, particularly jute mill workers in Bengal, and to promote education and moral upliftment among them. **So, pair I is** **not correctly matched.**
● **Durgeshnandini** is a classic literary work by **Bankim Chandra Chatterjee**. It was written in **Bengali** and is a romantic novel set against a historical backdrop. The narrative revolves around Tilottama, the daughter of a Bengali feudal lord; Jagat Singh, a Mughal general; and Ayesha, the daughter of a rebel Pathan leader who is in conflict with Jagat Singh. **So, pair II is correctly matched.**
● The **Mahratta** was a weekly English newspaper started by **Bal Gangadhar Tilak** alongside Vishnushastri Chiplunkar, M.B. Namjoshi, Vamanrao Apte and Gopal Ganesh Agarkar from Mumbai (erstwhile Bombay) in Maharashtra. It was first published on the 2nd of January, 1881 it came along with its brother organ the **Kesari,** another Marathi newspaper by **Tilak.** Newspapers like Mahratta were set in the background of the Extremist movement of the nationalist struggle for independence. **So, pair III** **is not correctly matched.**
● **Nibandhmala,** or **"Garlands of Essay,"** was an influential periodical started by **Vishnushastri** **Chiplunkar** in 1874. Many articles on numerous themes, including contemporary issues of the country, Sanskrit literature, the present condition of Marathi, etc., were published in the periodical. The periodical also went into great detail about the country's history and glorious past, as well as its significance in nation-building. **So, pair IV is not correctly matched.**
**So, only one of the pairs given above is correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
● Yayati is a **Marathi novel** written by **Shri Vishnu Sakharam Khandekar** and published in **1959**. Through a reinterpretation of the life of **King Yayati**, the son of the Chandravanshi king **Nahush**, the author presents a **critical reflection of** **contemporary society**. The novel’s literary significance was recognised with the **Jnanpith Award in 1960** and the **Sahitya Akademi Award in 1974**.

questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
**Statement I:** The Mobile Phone Operator Market in India is an example of a monopolist market structure.
**Statement II:** The Mobile Phone Operator Market in India is highly concentrated, with a few operators shaping supply and pricing.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?

options.a: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct and Statement II explains Statement I
options.b: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct but Statement II does not explain Statement I
options.c: Statement I is correct but Statement II is incorrect
options.d: Statement I is incorrect but Statement II is correct

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Market structure** refers to how different industries are classified and differentiated based on their degree and nature of competition for services and goods. The four popular types of market structures include **perfect** **competition, oligopoly market, monopoly market, and monopolistic competition**.
● An **oligopoly** market consists of a **small number of large companies** that sell differentiated or identical products. Since there are **few players** in the market, their competitive strategies are dependent on each other. **The** Mobile Phone Operator Market in India **is an oligopoly,** defined by intense competition among a **few dominant private players**—specifically Reliance Jio, Bharti Airtel, and Vodafone Idea—along with state-owned BSNL/MTNL. **So, statement I is not correct and** **statement II is correct.**
● In a **monopoly** market, a single company represents the whole industry. It has no competitor, and it is the sole seller of products in the entire market. This type of market is characterized by factors such as the sole claim to ownership of resources, patent and copyright, licenses issued by the government, or high initial setup costs. The company, therefore, remains a single seller because it has the power to control the market and set prices for its goods. **So, statement I is incorrect but Statement II is correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box:**
**What are the other types of market structures?**
● **Perfect competition** occurs when there is a large number of small companies competing against each other. They sell similar products (homogeneous), lack price influence over the commodities, and are free to enter or exit the market. Consumers in this type of market have full knowledge of the goods being sold. They are aware of the prices charged on them and the product branding.
● **Monopolistic competition** refers to an imperfectly competitive market with the traits  of both the monopoly and competitive market. Sellers compete among themselves and can differentiate their goods in terms of quality and branding to look different. In this type of competition, sellers consider the price charged by their competitors and ignore the impact of their own prices on their competition.

questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard
to the Stablecoins and Central Bank Digital
Currencies (CBDC):
I. - Unlike CBDCs, Stablecoins are issued by
private companies.
II. 	 Unlike CBDCs, Stablecoins were designed
for transactions in crypto markets.
III. 	 Both the Stablecoin and CBDC transactions
give central banks greater regulatory
oversight over financial flows.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
Stablecoins and Central Bank Digital Currencies (CBDCs) differ fundamentally in issuer, purpose, control, and accessibility.
● **Stablecoins are created by private companies or decentralized protocols** and are **not legal** **tender;** their stability depends on the issuer’s reserves and market confidence. In contrast, **CBDCs** **are issued and backed by a nation’s central bank**, making them official digital legal tender. **So,** **statement I is correct.**
● **Stablecoins** primarily aim to enable fast, low-cost digital transactions, especially in **cryptocurrency** **markets and cross-border payments**. CBDCs, however, are policy tools designed to modernize payment systems, **promote financial inclusion**, and enhance monetary policy effectiveness and regulatory oversight.  **So, statement II is correct.**
● In terms of transparency, stablecoin transactions on public blockchains can be visible but still rely on **trust in issuer disclosures**. **CBDCs provide stronger traceability and government** **supervision,** allowing central banks better visibility over financial flows. Finally, stablecoins are globally accessible through online wallets and operate continuously, whereas CBDCs are generally limited to domestic use under national regulations, often requiring user identification. **So, statement III is not** **correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: International Relations
questions.microTopic: India and the World (USA, Russia, Middle East, Africa, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to
the flow of inward remittances to India:
I. - The United States is the largest contributor to
India’s remittances, driven largely by Indian
professionals in high-remuneration sectors.
II. 	 The dominance of Gulf-based blue-collar
remittances to India has declined relative
to remittances from skilled migrants in
advanced economies.
Which of the statements given above is/are
correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● India’s demographic advantage is the youngest among major economies. This young population is increasingly skilled, mobile, and globally competitive, which is reflected in where Indian migrants work and earn today.
● Earlier, India’s remittance inflows **were dominated by blue-collar workers in Gulf countries**, particularly in construction and low-to-mid skill services. While these flows remain important, their **relative dominance has declined**. **So, statement II is correct.**
● A structural shift is now visible. During 2023–24, **more than 50% of India’s remittances came from** **advanced economies such as the United States**, United Kingdom, Singapore, Canada, and Australia, according to Reserve Bank of India data. These countries are magnets for white-collar, highskill employment, indicating that Indian migrants are increasingly employed in professional, managerial, scientific, and creative fields.
● The **United States emerged as the largest source**, contributing 27.7% of total remittances in 2023– 24, up from 22.9% in 2016–17. About 78% of Indian migrants in the US work in **high-remuneration** **sectors, and the expanding foreign-born workforce there has amplified remittance flows**. **So, statement I is correct.**
● Similarly, the UK’s share rose sharply from 3% to 10.8%, aided by policy frameworks like the India-UK Migration and Mobility Partnership. Countries such as Canada, Singapore, and Australia also gained prominence, reflecting education-to-employment migration pathways.
● At the same time, **Gulf Cooperation Council (GCC) countries still accounted for 38% of** **remittances, but their share has declined**. The UAE, once the top source, saw its share fall from
26.9% to 19.2%, signalling diversification rather than decline. **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Economy
questions.microTopic: GDP, Inflation, Unemployment
questions.questionText:
In 2026, while the global economy is expected
to remain resilient, several major European
economies are at high risk of a technical recession.
In the above context, which one of the following
best explains the concept of 'Technical Recession'?

options.a:
A slowdown characterised by short-term
output compression without a broad-based
decline in employment
options.b:
A slowdown associated with sharp currency
depreciation leading to higher import costs
and macroeconomic instability
options.c:
A phase of economic contraction triggered
by trade wars, rising protectionism, and
the imposition of trade barriers that disrupt
global trade flows
options.d:
A situation in which an economy records
negative growth in real Gross Domestic
Product for two consecutive quarters

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● A **technical recession is a term used to describe two consecutive quarters of decline in** **output**. In the case of a nation’s economy, the term usually refers to back-to-back contractions in real Gross Domestic Product.
● The most significant difference between a ‘technical recession’ and a **‘recession’** is that while the former term is mainly used to capture the trend in GDP, the latter **expression encompasses an appreciably** **more broad-based decline in economic activity** that covers several economic variables including employment, household and corporate incomes and sales at businesses.
● Another key feature of a technical recession is that it is **most often caused by a one-off event, for** **example,** the COVID-19 pandemic and the lockdowns imposed to combat it. It is **generally shorter** **in duration**.
● In 2026, the global economy is generally expected to show resilience with projected growth of approximately 2.7% to 3.3%, driven by technology investment, AI, and monetary policy easing. However, this outlook is accompanied by significant risks, with "stagflation light" (low growth, high inflation) threatening to push several major European economies and specific developing nations into a technical recession. **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** The global economy is largely avoiding a synchronised technical recession, but several major economies are currently experiencing stagnation or anaemic growth.

questions.questionText:
The central banks are among the world’s most
significant gold holders, in the above context,
arrange the following countries in the ascending
order of their gold holdings as a percentage of
their total reserves:
I. - United States of America (USA)
II. 	 China
III. 	 India
IV. 	 Japan
Select the correct answer using the code given
below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Gold continues to play a crucial role in the way central banks manage their reserves.** Valued for its safety, liquidity, and return characteristics, gold meets the three core investment objectives that guide reserve management. Because of this, **central banks are among the world’s most significant gold holders**, collectively accounting for around one-fifth of all the gold ever mined.
● The **United States of America (USA)** has a significantly higher amount of gold reserves than any other country, with **8,133.46 tonnes**. Gold accounts for **80.36% of all its reserves**, a testimony to the central role gold plays in the nation’s reserve composition despite lower foreign exchange reserves.
● **China’s** gold reserves stand at **2,303.51 tonnes**, forming **7.68% of its total reserves**. While the percentage share is modest, the absolute volume places China among the world’s top official gold holders.
● **Japan** reports **845.97 tonnes** of gold, representing **7.76% of its reserves**. As a high-income economy with substantial foreign exchange holdings, gold plays a stable but secondary role in its reserve structure.
● **India** holds **880.18** **tonnes** of gold, accounting for **15.17% of total reserves**. This relatively higher share reflects gold’s importance within India’s overall reserve diversification strategy. **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
